name: Skip Counting — Counting in Groups by 2s, 5s and 10s
whatIsIt: Skip counting is counting in equal JUMPS instead of by ones: by 2s (2, 4, 6, 8...), by 5s (5, 10, 15...), by 10s (10, 20, 30...). Counting things in equal groups is far faster than counting one by one — and it is the doorway to the times tables.
whyNeeded: When things come in equal groups — pairs of shoes, fingers in fives, ten-rupee notes — skip counting gets the total fast. It builds the rhythm of multiplication and makes counting big collections quick and reliable.
realLifeUses: Counting fingers or a hand of cards in 5s
Counting wheels on cars (4s) or legs on animals

vocabulary.meaning: Counting in equal jumps, like 2, 4, 6 instead of 1, 2, 3.
The equal amount you add each time (the group size).
Counting things that come in equal bundles.
The numbers you land on when skip counting: multiples of 5 are 5, 10, 15...

story.title: Sandals at the Temple Door
story.text: Outside the temple, sandals are lined up neatly in pairs. Meena starts '1, 2, 3...' and loses count. 'Sandals come in TWOS,' says the temple keeper. 'Jump by two: 2, 4, 6, 8, 10!' It is so much faster. 'Counting in equal groups is like hopping instead of walking,' Meena laughs.
story.extractedProblem: Five pairs of sandals are at the temple door. Counting in 2s, how many sandals is that?

visual.caption: Skip count by 2s: 2, 4, 6, 8, 10 — equal jumps on the number line.

teachingGallery.title: Skip counting — jump in equal groups
teachingGallery.note: Each jump adds the same amount. Watch the pattern grow!

standardMethod.summary: Pick the group size, then keep adding it: land on 2,4,6... or 5,10,15... or 10,20,30...
steps: See how big each equal group is (2, 5, or 10).
Keep adding the same amount for each new group.
Say the numbers you land on — those are the totals.

alternateMethods.name: Hop the number line
steps: Hop forward by the group size each time.
Read the number you land on.

alternateMethods.name: Groups-and-add
alternateMethods.whenToUse: To find a total of equal groups.
steps: Skip count by the group size that many times.
The last number is the total.

workedExamples.problem: Count 3 groups of 10 by skip counting.

commonMistakes.mistake: Skip counting with jumps that aren't equal (2, 4, 7, 9).
commonMistakes.fix: Every jump must add the SAME amount. By 2s it's always +2: 2, 4, 6, 8 — check each gap is equal.

commonMistakes.mistake: Starting a skip count from the wrong number (counting by 5 as 1, 6, 11).
commonMistakes.fix: Start ON the group size: by 5s begin at 5 (5, 10, 15), not at 1. The landing numbers are multiples of the jump.

commonMistakes.mistake: Skipping or repeating a step so the count goes wrong.
commonMistakes.fix: Add the jump carefully each time and don't rush. Use fingers or the number line to keep every jump in place.

medium.q: How many shoes are 4 pairs? (count by 2s)

challenge.q: 5 ten-rupee notes make how many rupees? (count by 10s)

questions.q: 3 pairs of socks = how many socks? (by 2s)

teachBackPrompt: Grab a pile of socks or coins. Teach someone at home to count them FAST by skip counting in 2s, 5s, or 10s — and show why the jumps must always be equal.

revisionCard.summary: Skip counting = equal JUMPS. By 2s: 2,4,6,8. By 5s: 5,10,15. By 10s: 10,20,30. Start on the group size and keep the jumps equal. It's the doorway to times tables.

gameMission.title: Counting the Shoes
gameMission.brief: Count piles of shoes, fingers and notes in fast equal jumps of 2, 5 and 10.

realLifeProject: Count all the shoes at your front door by 2s. Then count everyone's fingers in the room by 5s. Which was faster than counting by ones?

rememberIt.hook: Skip counting = counting in jumps: 2,4,6... or 5,10,15...
rememberIt.unpack: Count in 2s for pairs, 5s for hands and the clock, 10s for tens. Skip counting is the start of your times tables!

studentQuestions.a: It is faster for groups! Counting socks in 2s or fingers in 5s beats one-by-one, and it builds your times tables.

studentQuestions.q: Which is easiest to skip-count in?
studentQuestions.a: 10s (10,20,30) and 5s (5,10,15). Your fingers and toes help!
